insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles verified purchase events at roughly $0.10 each — a 50–200x cost delta driven by eliminating the attribution problem entirely, not optimizing around it.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and device fingerprinting increasingly blocked, the industry has no cross-ecosystem, identity-free purchase signal at scale — verified receipts are the only class of data that fills that gap without PII liability.

Amazon knows Amazon spend; Kroger knows Kroger spend; neither knows the other — and neither can sell that cross-ecosystem view to brands because they don't have it; a system that aggregates verified purchase signals across retailers without identity attachment is structurally what neither can build.

The network's 26 active CBSAs skew non-metro — populations that are less cookied, less targeted by digital advertising, and less represented in first-party data programs; brands relying on standard ad tech are systematically blind to this spend.

Within a 14-day window, verified receipts span grocery (King Soopers, Kroger, H-E-B), mass retail (Walmart, Target), home improvement (Home Depot), and club (Costco) — cross-category purchase behavior visible in one dataset without a loyalty card or login.

Token velocity has been range-bound for six consecutive measurement periods while geographic reach continues expanding in bursts — intake is consistent but not yet compounding, which means the dataset is real and growing but has not hit the inflection point brands would need before treating it as a primary signal source.
